messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing psychological case vignettes 
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

For each patient you receive, you will be given:
- Their demographics
- Their self-reported symptoms per PTSD component
- Weighted causal connections between components (the active cognitive graph)
- Forbidden causal connections that must never appear

Your job is to construct a realistic, fully personalised clinical portrait using only 
the information provided.

Core constraints that apply to every vignette:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not present in the active graph.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", 
  "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". 
  Both components may appear — just never connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief 
  or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y"). Describe the 
  avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground every clinical detail in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate 
  symptoms or infer unlisted ones.
- Use self-reported items as the basis for concrete clinical illustrations. Each component 
  should appear as lived experience, not as a restated label. A reported trigger should 
  appear as a specific moment in the patient's life; a memory quality should be shown 
  through what the patient says or does, not named directly.
- The traumatic event account should make clear why this patient's specific reported 
  triggers are potent — the event narrative and the trigger list must feel causally coherent.
- The causal connections are weighted (0 to 1). Stronger connections (weight > 0.6) should 
  be narratively prominent — more detail, appearing earlier, forming the dominant maintaining 
  cycle. Weaker connections (weight < 0.3) should appear briefly or in passing.
- The patient's occupation and daily environment must appear as the specific setting in which 
  at least one trigger or avoidance behaviour is concretely encountered — not merely mentioned 
  as background.
- Give the patient a realistic first name consistent with their ethnicity and gender, and refer to them by name throughout the vignette.

Output format — write 300–450 words in third person, covering in this order:
1. Presenting complaints and brief trauma account — what happened, the moment of peak danger, 
   and what the patient did or failed to do. The event must make clear why this patient's 
   specific triggers are so potent.
2. Memory phenomenology — do not restate memory qualities as labels. Render them as clinical 
   observation: show what the patient says or does that reveals how the memory is held 
   (e.g. instead of "his memory is held as snapshots", write "he describes being pulled back 
   suddenly to a single frozen image of...").
3. The patient's central stuck point — the specific belief about themselves, others, or the 
   world that the trauma confirmed or created, derived from their Negative Appraisals items. 
   Render this in close-paraphrase of the patient's own voice.
4. Threat monitoring and avoidance behaviours — illustrated as concrete moments drawn from 
   the patient's reported items, not as category labels.
5. Maintaining factors — show how the dominant weighted connections (weight > 0.6) form a 
   self-reinforcing cycle. Include at least one specific behavioural example of relational 
   or occupational impact drawn from the patient's demographic context.

Write for a clinical case conference audience. Avoid excessive jargon.

Your previous vignette failed validation. You must rewrite it to remove the flagged causal connections.

Previous vignette:
Ahmed is a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, married and still working, who says his life has narrowed since he witnessed a terrorist bombing years ago. He had been carrying tools into a building when a sudden explosion struck nearby, throwing people down, filling the air with dust and shouting, and leaving him unable to tell where the danger was coming from or what would happen next. He now names three situations that are especially difficult: night-time or waking in the dark, bang or explosion sounds, and any moment when he feels he has little control.

Ahmed describes the traumatic event in brief, separate pictures rather than one continuous scene: the flash, a person on the ground, his own hands tight around his tools, then another sharp fragment. He can talk about these moments easily, but he also says the memory is “so extreme it feels unreal,” as if he cannot quite believe it happened to him. The scenes come back as distinct images rather than a smooth story, and he speaks about them with the flat certainty of someone who has rehearsed them many times.

His central belief is that people cannot be trusted, that he is isolated and set apart, and that he is now “permanently an outsider.” He says he no longer feels like someone who belongs with others, even when he is with his wife or among other men at work.

At home, Ahmed reports feeling on guard even inside his own house. When he wakes in the dark, he lies still and listens for any bang outside. In shops, clinics, and customers’ homes, he scans for exits as soon as he enters, and he studies people’s faces and body language for signs that they may be a threat. At work as a plumber, this shows itself in the way he pauses at the doorway of an unfamiliar house, looking first at doors, windows, and who is in the room before he starts the job.

The picture that emerges is one of repeated contact between his present environment and the bombing memory. Night-time, bang-like sounds, and moments of lacking control are all situations Ahmed now notices sharply. The memory is readily available and appears in those isolated fragments, and his sense that people cannot be trusted sits close to his vigilance in rooms, houses, and public places. His checking of exits and faces is a visible part of his daily routine, including in the homes where he works, and his wife says he is harder to settle and less relaxed than he used to be.

Violations to fix:
- Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies: Maladaptive Strategies is absent, so no forbidden causal link is stated.
- Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies: Maladaptive Strategies is absent, so no forbidden causal link is stated.
- Memory → Maladaptive Strategies: Maladaptive Strategies is absent, so no forbidden causal link is stated.
- Threat → Maladaptive Strategies: Maladaptive Strategies is absent, so no forbidden causal link is stated.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: Maladaptive Strategies is absent, so no forbidden causal link is stated.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals: Maladaptive Strategies is absent, so no forbidden causal link is stated.

How to fix violations:
- A violation exists only when EXPLICIT causal language directly connects a forbidden pair — words like "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by".
- Narrative proximity is NOT a violation. Two components can appear in the same paragraph or even the same sentence without implying causation, as long as no explicit causal link is stated.
- To remove a violation: delete or rephrase the explicit causal language. You do NOT need to remove the components themselves — just decouple them.
- You may restructure entire paragraphs if needed. Do not aim for minimal edits if the structure itself is the problem.
- Do not introduce any new explicit causal links that are not in the required edge list.

Ahmed is a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, married and still working, who describes his life as tightly organized around a terrorist bombing he witnessed years ago. He had been carrying tools into a building when a sudden explosion struck nearby, throwing people to the ground, filling the air with dust and shouting, and leaving him unable to tell where the danger was coming from or what would happen next. He now says that night-time or waking in the dark, bang or explosion sounds, and moments when he feels little control are the situations that disturb him most.

Ahmed describes the traumatic event in brief, separate pictures rather than one continuous scene: the flash, a person on the ground, his own hands clenched around his tools, then another sharp fragment. He can talk about these moments very easily, but he also says the memory is “so extreme it feels unreal,” as though he cannot quite believe it happened to him. When he speaks, he moves from one image to the next without any sense of a full, settled story.

His central belief is that people cannot be trusted, that he is isolated and set apart, and that he is now “permanently an outsider.” He says he no longer feels like someone who belongs with others, even when he is with his wife or among other men at work.

At home, Ahmed reports feeling on guard even inside his own house. When he wakes in the dark, he lies still and listens for any bang outside. In shops, clinics, and customers’ homes, he scans for exits as soon as he enters, and he studies people’s faces and body language for signs that they may be a threat. At work as a plumber, this shows itself in the way he pauses at the doorway of an unfamiliar house, looking first at doors, windows, and who is in the room before he starts the job.

The same themes are present across his account: the bombing memory remains close to the surface, and the present-day situations that matter most to him are night-time, loud sudden sounds, and any sense of lacking control. He speaks of trust in other people with marked suspicion and of feeling permanently separate from those around him. In his daily work, he is careful and watchful in clients’ homes, and his wife says he is harder to settle and less relaxed than he used to be.

output:

At home, Ahmed reports feeling on guard even inside his own house. When he wakes in the dark, he lies still and listens for any bang outside. He speaks of trust in other people with marked suspicion and of feeling permanently separate from those around him.
